questions.question: Two different `gcc` binaries are installed on your machine. Which one runs when you type `gcc`?
options: The one with the highest version number
The first one found in PATH, scanned left to right
The one most recently installed
Whichever the operating system chooses at random
questions.explanation: The shell scans `PATH` left to right and runs the first executable file with a matching name. Version numbers and install order are irrelevant — only PATH order matters.

questions.question: Why does the course install Rust through `rustup` instead of `apt install rustc` or `brew install rust`?
options: rustup is the only way to install Rust
Distro package managers usually ship outdated Rust versions and can't manage multiple toolchains side by side
apt and brew don't support Rust
Cargo is not available through apt or brew
questions.explanation: Distro package managers freeze versions for distro release cycles, so their Rust is often a year+ stale. `rustup` is a toolchain manager: it tracks stable/beta/nightly, lets you pin per-project toolchains, and updates atomically.

questions.question: Your script needs to detect whether `python3` is installed without crashing if it isn't. Which check is most portable across shells and minimal containers?
options: `which python3`
`type python3`
`command -v python3 >/dev/null 2>&1`
`ls /usr/bin/python3`
questions.explanation: `command -v` is a POSIX shell built-in, so it works in stripped-down containers that don't ship `which`. `ls /usr/bin/python3` fails if Python is installed elsewhere (e.g., Homebrew on macOS puts it in `/opt/homebrew/bin`).

questions.question: A learner says: 'My CI passes but my laptop fails on the same code.' Which is the single most likely cause given what this lesson covered?
options: The CI runs on faster hardware
The CI uses a different toolchain version (e.g., gcc 13 in CI vs gcc 11 locally)
The CI has more cores
GitHub caches the wrong build artifact
questions.explanation: Mismatched toolchain versions between CI and laptop are the most common cause of 'works in CI / fails locally' (or vice versa). Lesson takeaway: pin toolchain versions in CI *and* document them in your verify script.

questions.question: What is the purpose of running `--version` on every tool in the lesson's `verify-toolchain.sh`?
options: Aesthetic — just to make the table look nice
It signals whether the binary on PATH is actually functional (some installations break, leaving a binary that crashes on launch)
Required by POSIX for all tools
It updates the tool to the latest version
questions.explanation: A tool's presence on `PATH` does NOT prove it can run — corrupted installs, missing shared libraries, and ARM/x86_64 mismatches all produce binaries that exist but crash. Running `--version` and capturing the result is the cheapest live-fire test.

_meta.title: The Cs Toolchain What You Ll Install And Why
